participants.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official participant details on sample composition, eligibility, and lack of baseline differences, with the only discrepancy being the excluded participant which does not conflict with the reported total sample.
participants.extracted: The study included 55 mental health professionals and graduate students in mental health programs (psychology, counseling, social work, and educational psychology). Eligibility required a bachelor’s-level degree or higher. One participant was excluded because they could not complete the postworkshop assessment, leaving 54 participants for analysis. The sample was a diverse group of students and professionals, with no significant baseline differences between the two workshop conditions in demographic characteristics or pretest measures.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official intervention content, methods, and comparison condition closely, with only minor wording differences.
interventions.extracted: The intervention was a structured child maltreatment reporting workshop for mental health professionals and graduate students. It used a PowerPoint presentation to teach definitions, prevalence, legal reporting requirements under Nevada and federal law, indicators of maltreatment, and empirically supported procedures for making a report. The workshop included a videotaped role-play demonstrating appropriate reporting skills, periodic pauses for group discussion, and guided discussion about how personal biases affect reporting behavior and how to involve caregivers appropriately in the reporting process. Participants then practiced the techniques in pairs using protocol checklists. The comparison intervention was an ethnic cultural sensitivity in therapy workshop that presented literature on ethnic cultural sensitivity, described a behavioral approach to cultural sensitivity, reviewed the SSICECTS and CECTS measures, showed a videotaped role-play, prompted discussion, and included paired practice of the modeled techniques.
interventions.official:
Name:child maltreatment reporting workshop (Alvarez 2010, p 215)
Contents:(i) how to involve caregivers in the reporting process, (ii) systematic dissemination of state and federal laws relevant to reporting suspected child maltreatment, (iii) common indicators of child maltreatment, (iv) common misconceptions resulting in failure to report suspected child maltreatment (Alvarez 2010, p 213), (v) empirically supported procedures to assist in making a child maltreatment report (Alvarez 2010, p 215

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include an ineligible third measure, clinical expertise in reporting suspected child maltreatment, which is not listed in the official protocol outcomes.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure three outcomes before and after the workshop intervention. First, knowledge of child maltreatment reporting laws was assessed using a 15-item multiple-choice inventory covering State and Federal reporting laws, with scores ranging from 0 to 15, measured at pretest and posttest. Second, recognition and intent to report suspected child maltreatment was assessed using an 8-vignette inventory presenting scenarios of physical abuse, neglect, sexual abuse, and emotional abuse; respondents rated likelihood of reporting on a 7-point Likert scale, with reverse scoring applied to non-reportable scenarios so that higher scores reflected greater accuracy, measured at pretest and posttest. Third, clinical expertise in reporting suspected child maltreatment was assessed using a 15-item multiple-choice inventory focused on skills relevant to safeguarding the therapeutic relationship during reporting, with scores ranging from 0 to 15, measured at pretest and posttest.
outcomes.official:
Eligible measures (outcome domain)
Recognition and intention to report suspected child maltreatment (primary outcome: number of reported cases of child abuse and neglect, measured subjectively by participant responses to vignettes), comprising 8 vignettes with response options on a 7‐point Likert‐type scale (Alvarez 2010, p 214)
